focusKeyword: how I funded my first business
content:
How I Funded My First Business: The Night Shift Brewing Story

In 2012, my co-founders Rob and Mike and I started Night Shift Brewing with roughly $180,000. Over 13 years, we grew it into one of New England's largest independent craft breweries - 150+ employees, multiple locations, a distribution company, and eventually an acquisition. But the beginning wasn't a straight line. It was a financial education delivered at the speed of "oh no, we're running out of money."

This is the honest version of how we funded it, what we got wrong, and what I'd tell anyone raising money for their first business today.

Where the $180,000 Came From

We didn't have rich parents or trust funds. The money came from three sources: personal savings (the majority), a small friends-and-family round, and credit cards (not recommended, but honest). We each put in what we could afford to lose - which is the only honest framework for personal investment in a startup. If losing this money would devastate you financially, you're over-investing.

We deliberately didn't take a bank loan or SBA loan at the start. Not because we couldn't qualify, but because we wanted to prove the concept before taking on debt with personal guarantees. In hindsight, this was the right call - we made enough mistakes in year one that having a $200,000 loan payment on top of everything else might have broken us.

What the $180,000 Covered

Brewing equipment (the big cost), a lease on a small industrial space, licensing and permits (federal TTB approval alone took 6 months), initial ingredients, packaging, a minimal taproom buildout, and a small marketing budget. Our buildout came in about 30% over our original estimate - which, I've since learned, is completely normal for food and beverage buildouts.

What it didn't cover: three months of operating expenses before we had meaningful revenue. That gap was the scariest part of the entire journey. We were paying rent, buying ingredients, and running the taproom while bringing in $3,000-$5,000/week. The math didn't work until month 4, and even then it was tight.

The Three Biggest Financial Mistakes

Mistake 1: Underestimating buildout costs by 30%. Every contractor quote was the optimistic version. Plumbing was more than quoted. Electrical needed upgrades we didn't anticipate. The health department required modifications we didn't plan for. By the time we opened, our buildout had consumed 30% more than budgeted - and that extra money came from the operating cash we'd set aside for the first three months.

Mistake 2: Not accounting for the cash flow timing gap. We bought ingredients in week 1, but the beer didn't sell until week 6 (because it takes weeks to brew, condition, and package). Meanwhile, rent, utilities, and staff costs were due immediately. This gap - spending money today on revenue you won't see for weeks or months - nearly killed us. Every product-based business faces some version of this, and most entrepreneurs underestimate it.

Mistake 3: Waiting too long to raise prices. We launched at prices that were competitive but left almost no margin for error. By the time we realized our cost of goods was higher than projected (ingredients, packaging, and waste were all worse than our spreadsheet assumed), we'd established price expectations with customers that were hard to change. We should have launched 15% higher and adjusted down if needed, rather than launching low and trying to raise prices with an established customer base.

What I'd Do Differently Today

Start smaller. We tried to launch as a "real brewery" from day one - commercial-scale equipment, a taproom, distribution plans. If I did it again, I'd start with the smallest viable version (a nanobrewery or even a contract brewing arrangement), prove the product and the market, and scale with revenue instead of investment capital.

Have 30% more cash than I think I need. Our $180,000 should have been $230,000. The extra $50,000 in reserves would have eliminated the panic of months 2-4 and let us make better decisions from a position of stability rather than survival.

Hire an accountant from month one, not month six. We tracked expenses in a spreadsheet that gradually became incomprehensible. By the time we hired a real accountant, reconstructing our books took weeks and cost more than having done it right from the start.

The Lesson That Applies to Every Business

Your startup cost is not just the number you spend to open. It's that number plus the cash you need to survive until the business can sustain itself. For Night Shift, the opening cost was $180,000, but the real cost - including the cash flow gap, the buildout overruns, and the slow first months - was closer to $250,000.

Whatever business you're starting, calculate two numbers: the cost to open and the cost to survive your first 3-6 months of operations with less revenue than you hope for. The second number is the one that matters, and it's the one most people forget.

Night Shift survived. Grew. Thrived. Was eventually acquired. But it survived those first few months not because our plan was perfect - it survived because we had just barely enough cash to outlast our mistakes. Give yourself more room than "barely enough." Your future self will thank you.
